{{Infobox ancient site |name = Otozuka Kofun |image = [[File:Otozuka Kofun Door.jpg|280px]]
Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular burial mound. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large burial mound in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Otozuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passageway (toward the burial chamber) Otozuka Kofun from the side.jpg|Mound == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large burial mound in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular burial mound. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large burial mound in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. {{Infobox ancient site |name = Otozuka Kofun |image = [[File:Otozuka Kofun Door.jpg|280px]]
Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular burial mound. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large burial mound in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Otozuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Burial chamber (toward the rear wall) Otozuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Burial chamber (toward the entrance) Otozuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passageway (toward the entrance) Otozuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passageway (toward the burial chamber) Otozuka Kofun from the side.jpg|Mound == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun - Designated December 14, 1938 (Shōwa 13), with additional areas designated on October 13, 2017.{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1270|Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Toki City Mino Ceramic History Museum|ja|土岐市美濃陶磁歴史館}} (Izumi-cho, Kuzuri, Toki City) - Holds artifacts from Otozuka Kofun. == References == {{References Help}} {{reflist}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Otozuka Kofun}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1270|Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun}} * [https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website) Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular burial mound. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large burial mound in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Otozuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Burial chamber (toward the rear wall) Otozuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Burial chamber (toward the entrance) Otozuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passageway (toward the entrance) Otozuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passageway (toward the burial chamber) Otozuka Kofun from the side.jpg|Mound == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun - Designated December 14, 1938 (Shōwa 13), with additional areas designated on October 13, 2017.{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1270|Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Toki City Mino Ceramic History Museum|ja|土岐市美濃陶磁歴史館}} (Izumi-cho, Kuzuri, Toki City) - Holds artifacts from Otozuka Kofun. == References == {{References Help}} {{reflist}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Otozuka Kofun}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1270|Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun}} * [https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website) Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. {{Infobox ancient site |name = Otozuka Kofun |image = [[File:Otozuka Kofun Door.jpg|280px]]
Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular burial mound. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large burial mound in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. {{Infobox ancient site |name = Otozuka Kofun |image = [[File:Otozuka Kofun Door.jpg|280px]]
Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular burial mound. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large burial mound in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Otozuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Burial chamber (toward the rear wall) Otozuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Burial chamber (toward the entrance) Otozuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passageway (toward the entrance) Otozuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passageway (toward the burial chamber) Otozuka Kofun from the side.jpg|Mound == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun - Designated December 14, 1938 (Shōwa 13), with additional areas designated on October 13, 2017.{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1270|Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Toki City Mino Ceramic History Museum|ja|土岐市美濃陶磁歴史館}} (Izumi-cho, Kuzuri, Toki City) - Holds artifacts from Otozuka Kofun. == References == {{References Help}} {{reflist}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Otozuka Kofun}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1270|Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun}} * Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. {{Infobox ancient site |name = Otozuka Kofun |image = [[File:Otozuka Kofun Door.jpg|280px]]
Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular burial mound. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large burial mound in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. {{Infobox ancient site |name = Otozuka Kofun |image = [[File:Otozuka Kofun Door.jpg|280px]]
Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular burial mound. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large burial mound in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular Kofun. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large Kofun in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. {{Infobox ancient site |name = Otozuka Kofun |image = [[File:Otozuka Kofun Door.jpg|280px]]
Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular Kofun. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large Kofun in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large Kofun in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular Kofun. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large Kofun in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Otozuka Kofun |image = [[File:Otozuka Kofun Door.jpg|280px]]
Mound and stone chamber entrance |Alternative name = |Affiliation = |location = {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Izumi-cho, Kuzuri 1332 (Shōbu) and surrounding areas |Latitude degree = 35|Latitude minute = 21|Latitude second = 55.93 |Longitude degree = 137|Longitude minute = 10|Longitude second = 51.35 |ISO = JP-21 |type = {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 27.4 m × 26.1 m
Height: 5.8 m |Burial Facility = Both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = Iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} |Accompanying Mounds = |Construction Period = Early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Interred Person = |Imperial Tomb = |Historic Site = National Historic Site "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun" |Tangible Cultural Properties = |Notable Points = |Map = Japan Gifu |Icon = Hōfun |Label = Otozuka Kofun |Label Position = Top }} '''Otozuka Kofun''' (乙塚古墳) is a {{ill|Hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Toki City|ja|土岐市}}, Gifu Prefecture. Together with the nearby {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}}, it is designated as a National Historic Site (official designation: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"). == Overview == [[File:Otozuka-danjirimaki 1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|{{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} (left) and Otozuka Kofun (right, in the background)}}]] Situated in southeastern Gifu Prefecture, on a river terrace along the northern bank of the {{ill|Toki River|ja|土岐川}}, at an altitude of about 150 meters, Otozuka Kofun is a large-scale rectangular Kofun. Locally, it is traditionally associated with Otomehime, the daughter of {{ill|Yasaka Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|八坂入彦命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. Excavations have been conducted since 2002 (Heisei 14). The mound is rectangular in shape (previously thought to be circular before the 2002 survey) and measures 27.4 meters (north-south) by 26.1 meters (east-west) with a remaining height of 5.8 meters. There is no evidence of stepped construction or {{ill|Fukiishi|ja|葺石}} on the mound's surface, nor of a surrounding moat. The burial facility is a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening south-southeast. The total length of the chamber is 19.2 meters, making it one of the largest stone chambers in the Mino region. Most of the grave goods have been lost, but fragments of iron products, {{ill|Sue Ware|ja|須恵器}}, and {{ill|Haji Ware|ja|土師器}} have been discovered. Among these, a decorative Sue Ware vessel featuring a bird-shaped knob is particularly noteworthy due to its rarity. The construction of the mound is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, at the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. It is thought to have been built around the same time as Danjirimaki Kofun. Otozuka Kofun represents a key example of a large Kofun in the Higashi-Mino region, likely serving as the tomb of a powerful local leader governing the area known as Tōki no Kōri (later {{ill|Toki District|ja|土岐郡}}). Its rectangular design suggests ties to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, providing insight into regional governance and power structures during the period.[https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website). The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 (Shōwa 13). Although it is open to the public following conservation efforts, access to the stone chamber is restricted and permitted only on the second Sunday of each month. == Burial Facility == [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Otozuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone chamber layout}}]] The burial facility consists of a both-sleeved {{ill|Yokoana-style Stone Chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, opening to the south-southeast. Dimensions of the chamber are as follows: * Total length: 19.2 meters * Burial chamber: Length 5.1 meters, width 1.9–2.7 meters, height 2.7–3.0 meters * Passageway: Length 5.4 meters, width 2.3–2.6 meters, height 2.4–2.7 meters * Forecourt: Length 8.1 meters, width 2.6–4.2 meters The stone materials are primarily quarried granite from nearby sources, with chert used for infill stones and gravel flooring. The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance. Otozuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Burial chamber (toward the rear wall) Otozuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Burial chamber (toward the entrance) Otozuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passageway (toward the entrance) Otozuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passageway (toward the burial chamber) Otozuka Kofun from the side.jpg|Mound == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun - Designated December 14, 1938 (Shōwa 13), with additional areas designated on October 13, 2017.{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1270|Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun}} == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Toki City Mino Ceramic History Museum|ja|土岐市美濃陶磁歴史館}} (Izumi-cho, Kuzuri, Toki City) - Holds artifacts from Otozuka Kofun. == References == {{References Help}} {{reflist}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Danjirimaki Kofun|ja|段尻巻古墳}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Otozuka Kofun}} * {{National Cultural Properties Database|401|1270|Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun}} * [https://www.city.toki.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/1004852/1004853/1006503/1003298.html National Historic Site: "Otozuka Kofun and Danjirimaki Kofun"] (Toki City Website) [[Category:Kofun in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:History of Toki City]] [[Category:Hōfun]] [[Category:National Historic Sites in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Otozuka Kofun]][[ja:乙塚古墳]] [[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Otozuka Kofun}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Otozuka Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

The burial chamber has a barrel-shaped plan, with a rear wall composed of a single large stone topped with smaller stones, and side walls constructed in three layers. The entrance features a lintel stone supported by standing pillars. The chamber floor, partially restored to about 20 cm above its original level, includes a drainage channel filled with small gravel leading from the passageway to the forecourt. The ceiling comprises three stones in the burial chamber and four in the passageway. Most grave goods have been lost, but the discovery of a decorative Sue Ware vessel with a bird-shaped knob highlights the site's cultural significance.
